progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The manuscript introduces an intriguing intersection between mechanism design and multi-agent reinforcement learning, proposing a "Truthful Self-Play" (TSP) framework to mitigate deception and information asymmetry in partially observable environments. The paper is bold in its claims, asserting both theoretical convergence guarantees and state-of-the-art empirical performance across complex benchmarks like StarCraft. However, the current exposition suffers from significant clarity issues; the writing is often imprecise or grammatically strained (e.g., "the state-of-the-art performance of our framework"), and the distinction between the proposed "imaginary rewards" and the underlying peer prediction mechanism requires much more rigorous grounding. For a work promising to bridge game-theoretic concepts (truthfulness, mechanism design) with deep learning, the transition between these fields feels loosely coupled. To improve the paper, the authors must tighten the formal definitions, provide a clearer intuition for why these "imaginary" gradients do not introduce instability, and reconcile the ambitious theoretical claims with the practical, often noisy, realities of training deep recurrent policies in multi-agent settings.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim of global convergence
comments.quote: TSP is a general optimization algorithm whose convergence is theoretically guaranteed for arbitrary policies and environments.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their algorithm has guaranteed convergence to the global optimum. However, they are training deep neural networks (communicative recurrent agents) using policy gradient methods (REINFORCE) in a non-cooperative partially observable stochastic game (Comm-POSG). Standard reinforcement learning in non-cooperative multi-agent settings generally faces convergence to local equilibria (e.g., BNE), not necessarily global optima, and convergence guarantees for deep RL are typically non-existent due to the non-convex nature of the objective landscape. While they claim to modify the gradient at local optima via imaginary rewards to help 'reflect the bias... on the gradient', this does not constitute a rigorous proof of global convergence.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency: J*[G] = J^[G]
comments.quote: In general, J[∗] = J_[ˆ] holds, which is closely related to the information asymmetry.
comments.explanation: The text states that the social welfare at the BNE (J[∗]) equals the global maximum (J^[ˆ]). This is mathematically inaccurate for the general class of non-cooperative games defined. The paragraph immediately preceding this sentence acknowledges information asymmetry, which is precisely the cause of the gap between BNE and social optimum. The statement contradicts the paper's premise that they are solving a problem where agents settle for 'bad local optima.' If J[∗] always equaled J^[ˆ], there would be no problem to solve.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency regarding summation indices
comments.quote: the mean of the cross entropy between the probability distributions of each model will be minimized... Dφ ( Zt ) = H [ p ( st )]
comments.explanation: In Proposition 3.1, the authors define a condition for global optimality involving Dφ, but Dφ is not formally defined in the provided sections (only mentioned as a 'lower bound' in text). Additionally, the summation indices in the preceding (unlabeled) objective function definition for social welfare are missing their limits, and the notation J[π][i] is introduced without defining if the brackets denote agents or specific components of the value function, leading to potential confusion when navigating the later proofs.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'imaginary' reward mechanism definition
comments.quote: the sum of the imaginary rewards, i.e., imaginary part of the social welfare, is zero.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'imaginary rewards' using the complex unit i, but the mechanism for mapping these to a real-valued neural network update is underspecified. They imply that the 'mass parameter β' allows the network to maintain a 'real structure,' but it is unclear how the complex-valued gradient is projected back onto the real-valued parameter space of the neural network (e.g., is it just the real part? Or does the imaginary part act as a penalty term?). For an optimization algorithm, this mapping is critical for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Peer Prediction Logic for Latent States
comments.quote: We apply the mechanism to RL, i.e., the problem of predicting the agent’s optimal behavior $a_{ti} \sim \pi_\theta|s_t$ for the true state $s_t \in S$.
comments.explanation: The text states the objective is to predict the optimal behavior $a_{ti} \sim \pi_\theta|s_t$ given true state $s_t$. However, earlier sections emphasize that $s_t$ is partially observable and unknown to any individual agent. While the authors argue the peer prediction mechanism handles unknown events by using signals ($z$) to infer types, this specific sentence posits that the peer prediction is evaluating $a_{ti}$ relative to $s_t$. If $s_t$ is inaccessible, the scoring rule $F$ cannot be conditioned on $s_t$ directly. The justification for how this is possible without an oracle or ground truth remains underspecified, creating a potential logical disconnect in the stated application of the peer prediction mechanism.

comments.title: Undefined Mathematical Operation in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Receive the message $\hat{\hat{z}}_{ti} \leftarrow \mathbf{Z}^T_t : i$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbf{Z}^T_t : i$ (likely meaning the relevant column or slice of the signal matrix) is introduced abruptly here. While it is clear from context that this refers to the messages received by agent $i$ from all other agents, the notation is not formally defined in Section 3 (where $\mathbf{Z}_t$ was defined). Given that $\mathbf{Z}_t$ is an $n \times n$ matrix, the slice operation should be explicitly defined for the reader to reproduce the message-passing step, especially since the definition of the post-state model $q_\phi$ in Section 3.2 uses slightly different notation for the received message ($\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}$).

comments.title: Potential Conflict in Hermitian Matrix Requirement
comments.quote: We perform zero-averaging by subtrac
comments.explanation: The text cuts off while describing the construction of the score matrix. The prior discussion relies on the score matrix $L_t$ being part of an Hermitian matrix to ensure that the sum of imaginary rewards is zero (Proposition 4.1). However, the peer prediction scoring rule $F(p(s|z) \| s)$ is generally not symmetric; $F(i, j)$ (score of $i$ regarding $j$'s report) is not necessarily equal to the conjugate of $F(j, i)$. The claim that the system ensures Hermitianity relies on the 'zero-averaging' step mentioned in the final incomplete sentence. Because this is the core mechanism to ensure the 'imaginary' rewards do not bias the social welfare, leaving this step undefined leaves a major gap in the mathematical validity of the proposed framework.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in the definition of the graph Laplacian operator
comments.quote: This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian ∆:= E − 11[T] /n.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the variable 'n' is defined as the number of agents. If E is defined as the unit matrix and 1 is defined as (1, ..., 1)[T] (an n-dimensional column vector), then the outer product 11[T] results in an n x n matrix of ones. While the definition of \Delta seems to target the centering/zero-averaging operation commonly used in game theory, if E is the identity matrix, \Delta = I - J/n results in a matrix where diagonal elements are (1 - 1/n) and off-diagonal elements are -1/n. When this acts on an n x n score matrix Lt, the operation (I - 11^T/n)Lt would apply the transformation to the columns, but not the rows. For zero-averaging an n x n matrix to ensure sum-to-zero in both dimensions, a two-sided operation (e.g., \Delta Lt \Delta) is typically required to satisfy the complex-reward equilibrium condition. The current definition is underspecified for a square matrix transformation.

comments.title: Ambiguous index in scoring rule expansion
comments.quote: ℓ ( ati|ztji ) := F ( πθ ( ati|ztji ) ∥ati ) = log πθ ( ati|ztji )
comments.explanation: The notation ztji (signal from agent i to agent j) appears in the definition of the score matrix entry \ell. However, the score depends on the agent's internal belief distribution. The index ztji implies the signal received by agent i from agent j (or vice versa depending on convention). Given Equation 6 identifies the matrix Lt as coming from Agent i to Agent j, the variable \ell(a_t^i | z_t^{ji}) seems to mix the action of Agent i with a signal from, arguably, j to i. There is potential confusion here between whether the verifier is assessing their own signal or the signal received from others, which is critical for the peer-prediction mechanism to work.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding Hermitian property of the Laplacian-adjusted reward
comments.quote: R + i ∆ L is Hermitian
comments.explanation: The authors claim that R + i \Delta L is Hermitian. Given R is diagonal (real-valued) and i is the imaginary unit, for the complex reward to be Hermitian, the matrix (\Delta L) must be real-symmetric. However, the graph Laplacian \Delta adjusted matrix (\Delta L) is generally not symmetric unless Lt is symmetric. The authors do not provide a derivation or condition under which the score matrix Lt (which is constructed from peer predictions) is guaranteed to be symmetric, which is a non-trivial property in asymmetric signaling environments.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Imaginary Reward Definition
comments.quote: The whole imaginary reward is denoted as i Y = (i yij )i,j∈ n  where i yij is the imaginary reward passed from Agent i to Agent j, and the complex reward for the whole game is R[+] := R + i Y where R is a diagonal matrix
comments.explanation: The text defines the complex reward as R[+] := R + iY, where i is the imaginary unit and Y is the matrix of imaginary rewards. However, earlier in Section 1 (Notation & Definitions), the summary states: 'Imaginary Reward Implementation: i ∆ Lt'. Later in Section 4.2/4.3, it clarifies that the imaginary reward is derived from the score matrix Lt via the operation i ∆ Lt. The definition in Section 4.1, 'R[+] := R + iY', is ambiguous because Y is not explicitly defined as '∆ Lt' until the procedure in Algorithm 1 and the text in 4.3. Furthermore, the use of the bold 'i' for the imaginary unit (as noted in the author's footnote 2) is inconsistent with standard mathematical usage where 'i' is the unit, often leading to confusion with the index 'i'. While the footnote 2 acknowledges the dual use, it remains a significant risk for misinterpretation of the core equation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim on Global Optimum
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1: For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum J[^] if the convergence conditions for the bounded mass parameter β are met.
comments.explanation: The paper makes strong claims about convergence to the global optimum J[^]. While Proposition 4.1 establishes that if the game is 'truthful' and the reward matrix is Hermitian then the equilibrium coincides with the global optimum, the leap to Theorem 4.1 (that TSP converges to this optimum) assumes the peer prediction mechanism successfully enforces truthfulness in all Comm-POSG settings. Peer prediction methods (like Miller et al., 2005) typically require strong assumptions about the relationship between signals and states (e.g., that signals are verifiable or that agents share a specific common prior/signal structure). The transition from 'truthful reporting' to 'global optimum' in non-cooperative settings is non-trivial, and the paper does not provide a formal proof or sufficient boundary conditions (regarding the structure of the Comm-POSG) to guarantee that the agent policies will converge to the 'truthful' Bayesian Nash Equilibrium via the proposed local gradient update.

comments.title: Zero-Averaging Logic Ambiguity
comments.quote: Since the score matrix Lt does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtrac
comments.explanation: The text cuts off the description of the zero-averaging process. The summary context mentions the use of 'Graph Laplacian (∆) := E - 11[T] /n' to normalize the matrix. However, the connection between the non-Hermitian score matrix Lt and the Hermitian requirement for the complex reward R[+] (Proposition 4.1) is mathematically fragile. Simply subtracting the mean (zero-averaging) does not generally make a matrix Hermitian unless the scoring rule itself was symmetric or constructed to be skew-symmetric. If the resulting matrix is not skew-symmetric, the imaginary part of the social welfare will not be zero, violating the guarantee in Proposition 4.1.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency: J*[G] = J^[G]
comments.quote: In general, J[∗] = J_[ˆ] holds, which is closely related to the information asymmetry.
comments.explanation: The text states that the social welfare at the BNE (J[∗]) equals the global maximum (J^[ˆ]). This is mathematically inaccurate for the general class of non-cooperative games defined. The paragraph immediately preceding this sentence acknowledges information asymmetry, which is precisely the cause of the gap between BNE and social optimum. The statement contradicts the paper's premise that they are solving a problem where agents settle for 'bad local optima.' If J[∗] always equaled J^[ˆ], there would be no problem to solve.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency regarding summation indices
comments.quote: the mean of the cross entropy between the probability distributions of each model will be minimized... Dφ ( Zt ) = H [ p ( st )]
comments.explanation: In Proposition 3.1, the authors define a condition for global optimality involving Dφ, but Dφ is not formally defined in the provided sections (only mentioned as a 'lower bound' in text). Additionally, the summation indices in the preceding (unlabeled) objective function definition for social welfare are missing their limits, and the notation J[π][i] is introduced without defining if the brackets denote agents or specific components of the value function, leading to potential confusion when navigating the later proofs.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'imaginary' reward mechanism definition
comments.quote: the sum of the imaginary rewards, i.e., imaginary part of the social welfare, is zero.
comments.explanation: The authors introduce 'imaginary rewards' using the complex unit i, but the mechanism for mapping these to a real-valued neural network update is underspecified. They imply that the 'mass parameter β' allows the network to maintain a 'real structure,' but it is unclear how the complex-valued gradient is projected back onto the real-valued parameter space of the neural network (e.g., is it just the real part? Or does the imaginary part act as a penalty term?). For an optimization algorithm, this mapping is critical for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Peer Prediction Logic for Latent States
comments.quote: We apply the mechanism to RL, i.e., the problem of predicting the agent’s optimal behavior $a_{ti} \sim \pi_\theta|s_t$ for the true state $s_t \in S$.
comments.explanation: The text states the objective is to predict the optimal behavior $a_{ti} \sim \pi_\theta|s_t$ given true state $s_t$. However, earlier sections emphasize that $s_t$ is partially observable and unknown to any individual agent. While the authors argue the peer prediction mechanism handles unknown events by using signals ($z$) to infer types, this specific sentence posits that the peer prediction is evaluating $a_{ti}$ relative to $s_t$. If $s_t$ is inaccessible, the scoring rule $F$ cannot be conditioned on $s_t$ directly. The justification for how this is possible without an oracle or ground truth remains underspecified, creating a potential logical disconnect in the stated application of the peer prediction mechanism.

comments.title: Undefined Mathematical Operation in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: Receive the message $\hat{\hat{z}}_{ti} \leftarrow \mathbf{Z}^T_t : i$
comments.explanation: The notation $\mathbf{Z}^T_t : i$ (likely meaning the relevant column or slice of the signal matrix) is introduced abruptly here. While it is clear from context that this refers to the messages received by agent $i$ from all other agents, the notation is not formally defined in Section 3 (where $\mathbf{Z}_t$ was defined). Given that $\mathbf{Z}_t$ is an $n \times n$ matrix, the slice operation should be explicitly defined for the reader to reproduce the message-passing step, especially since the definition of the post-state model $q_\phi$ in Section 3.2 uses slightly different notation for the received message ($\hat{\mathbf{z}}_{ti}$).

comments.title: Potential Conflict in Hermitian Matrix Requirement
comments.quote: We perform zero-averaging by subtrac
comments.explanation: The text cuts off while describing the construction of the score matrix. The prior discussion relies on the score matrix $L_t$ being part of an Hermitian matrix to ensure that the sum of imaginary rewards is zero (Proposition 4.1). However, the peer prediction scoring rule $F(p(s|z) \| s)$ is generally not symmetric; $F(i, j)$ (score of $i$ regarding $j$'s report) is not necessarily equal to the conjugate of $F(j, i)$. The claim that the system ensures Hermitianity relies on the 'zero-averaging' step mentioned in the final incomplete sentence. Because this is the core mechanism to ensure the 'imaginary' rewards do not bias the social welfare, leaving this step undefined leaves a major gap in the mathematical validity of the proposed framework.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in the definition of the graph Laplacian operator
comments.quote: This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian ∆:= E − 11[T] /n.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the variable 'n' is defined as the number of agents. If E is defined as the unit matrix and 1 is defined as (1, ..., 1)[T] (an n-dimensional column vector), then the outer product 11[T] results in an n x n matrix of ones. While the definition of \Delta seems to target the centering/zero-averaging operation commonly used in game theory, if E is the identity matrix, \Delta = I - J/n results in a matrix where diagonal elements are (1 - 1/n) and off-diagonal elements are -1/n. When this acts on an n x n score matrix Lt, the operation (I - 11^T/n)Lt would apply the transformation to the columns, but not the rows. For zero-averaging an n x n matrix to ensure sum-to-zero in both dimensions, a two-sided operation (e.g., \Delta Lt \Delta) is typically required to satisfy the complex-reward equilibrium condition. The current definition is underspecified for a square matrix transformation.

comments.title: Ambiguous index in scoring rule expansion
comments.quote: ℓ ( ati|ztji ) := F ( πθ ( ati|ztji ) ∥ati ) = log πθ ( ati|ztji )
comments.explanation: The notation ztji (signal from agent i to agent j) appears in the definition of the score matrix entry \ell. However, the score depends on the agent's internal belief distribution. The index ztji implies the signal received by agent i from agent j (or vice versa depending on convention). Given Equation 6 identifies the matrix Lt as coming from Agent i to Agent j, the variable \ell(a_t^i | z_t^{ji}) seems to mix the action of Agent i with a signal from, arguably, j to i. There is potential confusion here between whether the verifier is assessing their own signal or the signal received from others, which is critical for the peer-prediction mechanism to work.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding Hermitian property of the Laplacian-adjusted reward
comments.quote: R + i ∆ L is Hermitian
comments.explanation: The authors claim that R + i \Delta L is Hermitian. Given R is diagonal (real-valued) and i is the imaginary unit, for the complex reward to be Hermitian, the matrix (\Delta L) must be real-symmetric. However, the graph Laplacian \Delta adjusted matrix (\Delta L) is generally not symmetric unless Lt is symmetric. The authors do not provide a derivation or condition under which the score matrix Lt (which is constructed from peer predictions) is guaranteed to be symmetric, which is a non-trivial property in asymmetric signaling environments.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Imaginary Reward Definition
comments.quote: The whole imaginary reward is denoted as i Y = (i yij )i,j∈ n  where i yij is the imaginary reward passed from Agent i to Agent j, and the complex reward for the whole game is R[+] := R + i Y where R is a diagonal matrix
comments.explanation: The text defines the complex reward as R[+] := R + iY, where i is the imaginary unit and Y is the matrix of imaginary rewards. However, earlier in Section 1 (Notation & Definitions), the summary states: 'Imaginary Reward Implementation: i ∆ Lt'. Later in Section 4.2/4.3, it clarifies that the imaginary reward is derived from the score matrix Lt via the operation i ∆ Lt. The definition in Section 4.1, 'R[+] := R + iY', is ambiguous because Y is not explicitly defined as '∆ Lt' until the procedure in Algorithm 1 and the text in 4.3. Furthermore, the use of the bold 'i' for the imaginary unit (as noted in the author's footnote 2) is inconsistent with standard mathematical usage where 'i' is the unit, often leading to confusion with the index 'i'. While the footnote 2 acknowledges the dual use, it remains a significant risk for misinterpretation of the core equation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim on Global Optimum
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1: For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum J[^] if the convergence conditions for the bounded mass parameter β are met.
comments.explanation: The paper makes strong claims about convergence to the global optimum J[^]. While Proposition 4.1 establishes that if the game is 'truthful' and the reward matrix is Hermitian then the equilibrium coincides with the global optimum, the leap to Theorem 4.1 (that TSP converges to this optimum) assumes the peer prediction mechanism successfully enforces truthfulness in all Comm-POSG settings. Peer prediction methods (like Miller et al., 2005) typically require strong assumptions about the relationship between signals and states (e.g., that signals are verifiable or that agents share a specific common prior/signal structure). The transition from 'truthful reporting' to 'global optimum' in non-cooperative settings is non-trivial, and the paper does not provide a formal proof or sufficient boundary conditions (regarding the structure of the Comm-POSG) to guarantee that the agent policies will converge to the 'truthful' Bayesian Nash Equilibrium via the proposed local gradient update.

comments.title: Zero-Averaging Logic Ambiguity
comments.quote: Since the score matrix Lt does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtrac
comments.explanation: The text cuts off the description of the zero-averaging process. The summary context mentions the use of 'Graph Laplacian (∆) := E - 11[T] /n' to normalize the matrix. However, the connection between the non-Hermitian score matrix Lt and the Hermitian requirement for the complex reward R[+] (Proposition 4.1) is mathematically fragile. Simply subtracting the mean (zero-averaging) does not generally make a matrix Hermitian unless the scoring rule itself was symmetric or constructed to be skew-symmetric. If the resulting matrix is not skew-symmetric, the imaginary part of the social welfare will not be zero, violating the guarantee in Proposition 4.1.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper introduces an intriguing framework—Truthful Self-Play (TSP)—that applies mechanism design to address information asymmetry in multi-agent reinforcement learning, a novel and potentially impactful direction. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity and rigor issues that undermine its contributions. The abstract and introduction make sweeping claims (“analytically demonstrate convergence to the global optimum,” “state-of-the-art performance”) without providing enough detail or evidence in the presented text; the theoretical analysis is only promised for later sections, and the experimental results are described at a superficial level. The writing contains numerous typos (“extention,” “pertially”), awkward phrasing (“Evolving culture prevents…,” “explored observation”), and an unnecessary notation section that includes complex numbers never used. More critically, the paper conflates self-play with evolutionary learning, uses the term “bias” imprecisely, and does not clearly delineate the problem setup (Comm-POSG) from prior work. The most significant issue is the lack of a concrete, rigorous formulation of how the imaginary reward via peer prediction is computed and how it modifies gradients—without this, the claimed theoretical guarantee of global convergence is unconvincing. The paper would benefit from sharper problem definition, a more focused narrative, and careful language to avoid overstatement.

comments.title: Questionable claim and likely improper citation for 'evolving culture' preventing local optima
comments.quote: Evolving culture prevents deep neural networks from falling into bad local optima (Bengio, 2012).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'evolving culture' is not a standard term in deep learning, and the citation to Bengio (2012) is suspect. Bengio's 2012 paper 'Deep Learning of Representations' (Journal of Machine Learning Research) discusses unsupervised feature learning and stacked autoencoders, but I am not aware of it introducing or supporting the idea that 'evolving culture prevents bad local optima'. The sentence appears to be a non-technical motivation, but the citation creates a factual claim. If the authors intended a different work (e.g., Bengio et al. on evolutionary dynamics or cultural evolution), the reference is ambiguous and potentially misleading. Even as introductory gloss, this overstates what has been shown and could mislead readers about the prior art. I flagged this as moderate because it undermines the credibility of the background framing and may indicate a broader lack of rigor in citing sources.

comments.title: Underspecified method: computation of score matrix _Lt_ in Algorithm 1 step 2
comments.quote: 2. Compute a score matrix _Lt_ with the peer prediction mechanism,
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, step 2 states 'Compute a score matrix _Lt_ with the peer prediction mechanism,' but the actual formula for _Lt_ is given much later in the text (equation (6) in the passage after section 3). The algorithm as presented is vague: it does not specify how to compute _Lt_ from the available variables (like actions, post-states, or signals). A reader trying to implement the algorithm would need to search for equation (6), which uses the logarithmic scoring rule based on _πθ(ati|ztji)_. However, even equation (6) is incomplete because it does not clarify how _πθ(ati|ztji)_ is evaluated when the agent's policy conditions on post-state _ĥti_, not directly on _ztji_. There is a mismatch: the policy is defined as _πθ(ati|ĥti)_ but in (6), the condition is on _ztji_. This is a genuine underspecification. The authors should explain how _πθ(ati|ztji)_ is derived from the CRA and whether it requires a separate forward pass through the network. This is a moderate issue because it affects reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing equation defining imaginary reward construction and insufficient justification for Hermitianity
comments.quote: Since the score matrix Lt does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero. This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian Δ:= E − 11[T] /n . to get which is the formula that connects reinforcement learning and mechanism design.
comments.explanation: The passage states that the imaginary reward is constructed from L_t using the graph Laplacian, but the final equation that expresses this construction is missing (the text ends with 'to get' and then nothing). Without this equation, the reader cannot determine how the imaginary reward matrix iY is formed from L_t, which is central to the TSP algorithm. The subsequent theorem and proof rely on this definition, so its absence makes the method underspecified and the claims unverifiable. Additionally, the authors conflate the property of zero-sum (imaginary part sums to zero) with Hermitianity of the augmented reward matrix R⁺ = R + iY. For R⁺ to be Hermitian, the real matrix Y must be skew-symmetric (Y^T = -Y). Zero-averaging alone does not impose skew-symmetry; it only ensures that the sum of all entries of Y is zero. The proof summary later claims that R + iΔL is Hermitian, which would require ΔL to be skew-symmetric. The text provides no justification that the centering operation (using Δ) yields a skew-symmetric matrix, and in general it does not. This logical gap undermines the theoretical claims (Proposition 4.1 and Theorem 4.1) that depend on R⁺ being Hermitian.

comments.title: Incomplete statement of Theorem 4.1 convergence condition
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1. (global optimally) For any games in Comm-POSG, TSP converges to the global optimum J[ˆ] if the following convergence condition are met, where β < ∞ is bounded mass parameter.
comments.explanation: The theorem states that TSP converges to the global optimum if 'the following convergence condition are met', but the condition itself is not provided in the passage. The reader is left to infer that it is given elsewhere (e.g., as Equation (9)), but within the text shown there is no specification. This makes the theorem statement incomplete and the convergence claim unsubstantiated in the excerpt.

comments.title: Questionable claim: 'the difference between evolutionary learning and supervised learning is the absence of human knowledge and oracles'
comments.quote: The difference between evolutionary learning and supervised learning is the absence of human knowledge and oracles.
comments.explanation: This is an oversimplification. Evolutionary learning (in the context of self-play) does not necessarily eliminate human knowledge; it eliminates human-labeled data, but the reward function, environment design, and algorithm hyperparameters still embed human knowledge. Also, supervised learning can be used in self-play (e.g., AlphaGo's initial training from human games). The statement is too broad and could mislead a reader into thinking that self-play is entirely free of human bias. However, this appears in the Related Work section, where such high-level assertions are common. It is not a core mathematical claim. I consider this a minor framing issue.

comments.title: Potential OCR misread: 'singals' instead of 'signals'
comments.quote: The _i_ -th diagonal entry of **Z** _t_ , _hti_ := _ztii_ represents the _pre-state_ , an internal state of _i_ -th agent before receiving the singals from the others.

comments.title: Unclear claim: 'All _n_ -agents share the same weight per episode'
comments.quote: All _n_ -agents share the same weight per episode, and the weights are updated based on the cumulative reward after the episode.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'share the same weight per episode' is ambiguous. It could mean that all agents have identical policy parameters at the start of an episode (i.e., parameter sharing common in MARL), or that the weights remain fixed throughout a single episode and are updated only between episodes. The former is a common practice in many multi-agent RL papers (e.g., parameter sharing in CommNet or IC3Net). The latter is standard for on-policy methods like REINFORCE. However, the phrasing 'per episode' could be misinterpreted as meaning the weights change every episode (which is true) but are shared among agents at any given time. I think the authors mean the standard parameter-sharing approach, but the wording is slightly imprecise. A careful reader might wonder if the weights are reset each episode or if all agents exactly share the same parameters. Given that this is a framework description and the actual algorithm in Section 4 uses standard policy gradient updates, no real ambiguity remains. Not flagged.

comments.title: Missing justification: zero-averaging step for score matrix is omitted
comments.quote: Since the score matrix _Lt_ does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtrac
comments.explanation: The sentence is cut off: 'we perform zero-averaging by subtrac...' (likely 'subtraction' or 'subtracting the mean'). The full algorithm in the passage before the section break mentions that 'the imaginary part of the social welfare, is zero' and that 'the system must be designed so that the sum of the imaginary rewards is zero.' However, the specific operation to make _Lt_ Hermitian (zero-averaging) is not defined. The reader is left to guess: do we subtract the row mean, column mean, or the global mean? Since the imaginary rewards _i yij_ need to satisfy _i yij = -i yji_ (skew-symmetric), a simple centering is not sufficient to guarantee Hermitianity (which in the real case is symmetry, not skew-symmetry). The authors claimed that **R**[+] should be Hermitian, which for purely imaginary off-diagonals means _i yij = -i yji_ (skew-symmetry). Zero-averaging (e.g., _ℓij - mean_) would preserve symmetry, not skew-symmetry. This is a significant gap: the transformation from a score matrix _Lt_ (asymmetric, not necessarily skew-symmetric) to an imaginary reward matrix **iY** that is Hermitian (i.e., skew-symmetric in the real part and symmetric in the imaginary part? Actually, Hermitian means _M_ij = conjugate of _M_ji. For purely imaginary off-diagonals, _i yij = -i yji_ implies _yij = -yji_, i.e., skew-symmetric. The text says 'zero-averaging by subtrac...' but does not specify the operation. The cut-off text likely continues in the next section (not shown). Without this, the algorithmic step is incomplete. This is a moderate issue.

comments.title: Non-standard use of 'graph Laplacian' for centering matrix
comments.quote: using the graph Laplacian ∆:= E − 11[T] /n
comments.explanation: The matrix I - (1/n)11^T is commonly called the centering or projection matrix, not the graph Laplacian. In graph theory, the Laplacian is defined as D - A (degree minus adjacency). Using 'graph Laplacian' here is unconventional and could confuse readers who expect the graph-theoretic definition. However, since the definition is explicitly given, the meaning is clear, so this is a minor terminology issue.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling motivation to address information hiding in non-cooperative partially observable multi-agent settings by integrating mechanism design (peer prediction) into self-play to incentivize truthful communication. The core idea of deriving auxiliary rewards from peer consistency is theoretically intriguing and has clear potential for improving cooperative representations. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity issues and raises critical concerns regarding its bold claims. Most notably, the assertion of "analytical convergence to the global optimum" is highly suspicious in the context of non-convex, multi-agent dynamics; the reviewer requests a rigorous examination of the proof's assumptions and applicability, as this claim appears disproportionate to the problem structure and may rely on restrictive conditions not evident in the text. Furthermore, the terminology surrounding "imaginary rewards" is ambiguous and potentially misleading; the notation section explicitly defines complex number operators (real and imaginary parts), which suggests the rewards may be complex-valued, yet the abstract and introduction treat them merely as virtual or auxiliary incentives without clarifying the role of complex arithmetic or why such notation is required. This disconnect requires immediate clarification. Finally, while the reported state-of-the-art results are promising, the evaluation relies on established benchmarks, and the comparison must ensure parity with contemporary MARL baselines to substantiate the practical efficacy of the proposed modification. Overall, while the direction is valuable, the paper requires substantial revision to resolve terminological inconsistencies and to critically defend its convergence guarantees before being considered for acceptance.

comments.title: Overclaim on theoretical convergence guarantees
comments.quote: TSP is a general optimization algorithm whose convergence is theoretically guaranteed for arbitrary policies and environments.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a standard introductory bold statement, but upon checking the theoretical foundations provided in the context (Propositions 3.1 and 4.1), I notice that the convergence guarantee relies on strict structural assumptions. Specifically, the proof requires the environment to be formalized as a Comm-POSG, the imaginary rewards to satisfy the Hermitian property (R+ is Hermitian), and the peer prediction mechanism to enforce truthfulness (minimizing D_φ). Stating that convergence is guaranteed for *arbitrary* policies and environments omits these crucial conditions, which fundamentally restrict the scope of the theoretical result. This overstatement could mislead readers regarding the algorithm's actual theoretical boundaries and applicability.

comments.title: Imprecise optimization terminology regarding gradients and optima
comments.quote: The mechanism affects the gradient of the local optima, but not the global optima.
comments.explanation: At first glance, the authors intend to explain that the imaginary reward term modifies the optimization landscape to help agents escape bad local minima without altering the value or location of the global optimum. However, the phrasing "gradient of the local optima" is mathematically imprecise. At any optimum (local or global), the gradient of the objective function is zero by definition. It makes more sense to say the mechanism affects the *gradient dynamics*, *optimization trajectory*, or *loss landscape* around optima. As written, the sentence could confuse readers who strictly interpret optimization terminology, and it obscures the actual mechanical role of the imaginary reward in the policy gradient update.

comments.title: Grammatical error disrupting logical flow
comments.quote: Because of this assumption, is it impossible to apply these models to a partially observable environment, where the distribution is not stable.
comments.explanation: This sentence contains a clear typographical/grammatical slip, reading "is it impossible" instead of the intended declarative "it is impossible." While likely a simple typo, the inverted structure momentarily turns the statement into a question, which disrupts the logical flow of the argument. A careful reader must re-parse the sentence to recover the intended meaning: that discriminative models (GANs, curriculum learning) cannot be applied in this setting due to the lack of stable distributions and supervision. Correcting the phrasing would improve readability and academic tone.

comments.title: Garbled sentence in Section 3.3
comments.quote: since achieving truthfulness is essentially the same problem as minimizing _Dφ_ , eventually it also maximizes _J[∗]_ will be maximized.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'eventually it also maximizes $J^*$ will be maximized' is grammatically broken and semantically redundant. It clearly intends to convey that minimizing $D_\phi$ (which bounds information asymmetry) consequently maximizes social welfare $J^*$. This is a drafting/OCR artifact that creates minor confusion but does not undermine the core claim.

comments.title: Index discrepancy in Algorithm 1 initialization
comments.quote: Genesis: _s_ 1 _∼ p_ ( _s_ )
comments.explanation: Definition 3.1 establishes the state evolution over $1 \le t \le T$ with an initial distribution $p(s_0)$. Algorithm 1 incorrectly initializes with $s_1 \sim p(s)$. Given the standard time-step convention and the formal definition provided, this should be $s_0 \sim p(s_0)$. The typo could mislead readers about the episode initialization and off-by-one indexing in the subsequent steps.

comments.title: Missing condition in Proposition 4.1 and its proof
comments.quote:
**Proposition 4.1.** _For any G in Comm-POSG, if G_ [ _**i**_ **Y** ] _is truthful and_ **R**[+] _is an Hermitian matrix, J[∗]_ ( _G_ [ _**i**_ **Y** ]) = _J_[ˆ] ( _G_ ) _holds._

_Proof._ Since _G_ [ _**i**_ **Y** ] is truthful, _J[∗]_ ( _G_ [ _**i**_ **Y** ]) = _J_[ˆ] ( _G_ [ _**i**_ **Y** ]) holds from Proposition 3.1.
comments.explanation: Proposition 3.1 explicitly requires that the reward function $R_i$ is symmetric under agent permutations for $J^* = \hat{J}$ to hold. Proposition 4.1 and its proof cite Proposition 3.1 but omit this symmetry requirement. Unless the construction of $iY$ inherently preserves or supersedes this condition (which is not argued), the logical deduction is incomplete. The authors should either restate the symmetry condition in Prop 4.1 or explain why it is satisfied in the modified game $G[iY]$.

comments.title: Conceptual gap in deriving the peer prediction score
comments.quote: $L_t := ( _\ell_ ( _ati|ztji_ )) _i,j∈_  _n_ ; _ℓ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) := _F_ ( _πθ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) _∥ati_ ) = log _πθ_ ( _ati|ztji_ ) _,_ (6), which is an _n_ -order square matrix representing the score from Agent _i_ to Agent _j_ .
comments.explanation: The authors invoke peer prediction and proper scoring rules to incentivize truthful belief reporting, but the implemented score reduces to $\ell = \log \pi_\theta(a_{ti}|z_{tji})$, which is simply the log-likelihood of the taken action under the policy given the received signal. Standard peer prediction (Miller et al., 2005) relies on cross-predicting other agents' reports to reward truthful information exchange without ground-truth supervision, exploiting correlation structures. The jump from mechanism design theory to a standard cross-entropy policy loss is unjustified here. It is unclear how this score leverages inter-agent correlations to guarantee truthfulness, making the methodological bridge to peer prediction questionable without further derivation.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding the mass parameter β
comments.quote: The real and imaginary parts of the complex rewards are ordered by the mass parameter _β_ during training
comments.explanation: The term 'ordered by' is non-standard in optimization contexts. Given that $\beta$ is listed as a hyperparameter in Algorithm 1's requirements, it almost certainly acts as a weighting or scaling coefficient (e.g., scaling the imaginary reward magnitude relative to real rewards). 'Weighted' or 'modulated' would be precise. As written, it introduces slight ambiguity about how $\beta$ mathematically enters the update rule, though the likely intent is straightforward regularization scaling.

comments.title: Conflation of Hermiticity and Zero-Sum properties
comments.quote: Since the score matrix _Lt_ does not satisfy Hermitianity, we perform zero-averaging by subtracting the mean of the scores from each element of the matrix, thereby making the sum to zero.
comments.explanation: The authors treat achieving a zero-sum matrix as equivalent to satisfying Hermitianity, which are mathematically distinct requirements. Hermitianity requires $M = M^*$ (i.e., $m_{ij} = \overline{m_{ji}}$), ensuring the imaginary part of the reward matrix is skew-Hermitian so that the imaginary component of expected social welfare vanishes. Zero-sumness only requires $\sum_{i,j} m_{ij} = 0$. Subtracting the mean of scores enforces the latter but does nothing to enforce the former. Since Proposition 4.1 explicitly requires $\mathbf{R}[+]$ to be Hermitian to guarantee that imaginary rewards do not alter real social welfare, this methodological shortcut breaks the theoretical link to global optimality.

comments.title: Overclaim of guaranteed global convergence
comments.quote: TSP is a general optimization algorithm whose convergence is theoretically guaranteed for arbitrary policies and environments.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that TSP guarantees convergence to the global optimum for arbitrary policies and environments. In multi-agent reinforcement learning, particularly with deep function approximation and non-convex objective landscapes, policy gradient methods are only theoretically guaranteed to converge to local optima or stationary points under specific assumptions (e.g., Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, convexity, or restricted function classes). Claiming global convergence for arbitrary deep learning settings ignores well-documented challenges such as saddle points, poor credit assignment, and non-stationarity. Theorem 4.1's phrasing ('converges to the global optimum $\hat{J}$ if the following convergence condition are met') further obscures this, as the condition is missing and the claim remains unjustified in standard RL theory.

comments.title: Misuse of 'graph Laplacian' terminology
comments.quote: This can be expressed as follows using the graph Laplacian ∆:= E − 11[T] /n .
comments.explanation: The matrix $\Delta = E - \frac{1}{n}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ is a standard centering matrix (or orthogonal projection onto the subspace orthogonal to the all-ones vector), not a graph Laplacian. While it acts as a mean-subtraction operator, calling it a 'graph Laplacian' is non-standard and potentially misleading, as graph Laplacians are typically defined as $L = D - A$ and carry specific spectral and connectivity interpretations. The intended mathematical operation (column-wise mean subtraction) is clear, but the terminology adds unnecessary confusion without altering the core algebraic manipulation.

comments.explanation: The citation entry for Milgrom et al. (1987) appears truncated or OCR-corrupted. It reads 'volume, pp. 32.', which omits the actual volume number (Vol. 2) and the correct page range (pp. 321–333). While likely an extraction artifact, it leaves a critical gap for readers attempting to locate the source material. Assuming the intended symbol was a full page range, the current text is unhelpful and technically incomplete as presented.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling motivation to address information hiding in non-cooperative partially observable multi-agent settings by integrating mechanism design (peer prediction) into self-play to incentivize truthful communication. The core idea of deriving auxiliary rewards from peer consistency is theoretically intriguing and has clear potential for improving cooperative representations. However, the manuscript suffers from significant clarity issues and raises critical concerns regarding its bold claims. Most notably, the assertion of "analytical convergence to the global optimum" is highly suspicious in the context of non-convex, multi-agent dynamics; the reviewer requests a rigorous examination of the proof's assumptions and applicability, as this claim appears disproportionate to the problem structure and may rely on restrictive conditions not evident in the text. Furthermore, the terminology surrounding "imaginary rewards" is ambiguous and potentially misleading; the notation section explicitly defines complex number operators (real and imaginary parts), which suggests the rewards may be complex-valued, yet the abstract and introduction treat them merely as virtual or auxiliary incentives without clarifying the role of complex arithmetic or why such notation is required. This disconnect requires immediate clarification. Finally, while the reported state-of-the-art results are promising, the evaluation relies on established benchmarks, and the comparison must ensure parity with contemporary MARL baselines to substantiate the practical efficacy of the proposed modification. Overall, while the direction is valuable, the paper requires substantial revision to resolve terminological inconsistencies and to critically defend its convergence guarantees before being considered for acceptance.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel framework, Truthful Self-Play (TSP), which integrates mechanism design concepts—specifically peer prediction—into evolutionary learning to mitigate information asymmetry and deception in non-cooperative environments. The motivation is compelling, addressing a fundamental instability in standard multi-agent self-play where agents converge to deceptive equilibrium rather than truthfulness. The writing is generally clear and establishes the problem setting well, though there are a few grammatical awkwardnesses (e.g., "Because of this assumption, is it impossible to apply these models..."). The most significant issue at this stage is the ambiguity regarding the definition and implementation of "imaginary rewards." In reinforcement learning, rewards must correspond to actual interaction outcomes; distinguishing between the observation signals processed by the agents and the rewards used for optimization requires precise mathematical formulation. Furthermore, the application of peer prediction, which typically relies on a centralized judge or strict reporting protocols, to a decentralized, fully observable environment must be rigorously defined to ensure the convergence claims in the theoretical section are valid. The authors should clarify how "social influence" is quantified in this decentralized setting before the formal convergence analysis begins.

comments.title: Suspicious Citation for Auction Theory
comments.quote: Paul Milgrom et al. Auction theory. In Advances in economic theory: Fifth world congress, volume 1, pp. 32. Cambridge Univ. Press Cambridge, 1987.
comments.explanation: While the rest of the bibliography is highly specific and accurate (e.g., exact ICLR/DIMACS volumes or correct journals), this entry for Paul Milgrom is highly likely to be incorrect. There is no known publication in 1987 by Paul Milgrom et al. titled simply 'Auction theory' in the 'Advances in economic theory: Fifth world congress' proceedings at Cambridge University Press. Paul Milgrom's seminal work on auctions is often cited in the context of the 1982 'Auction Theory' lecture notes or the Handbook of Industrial Organization. This entry appears to be a hallucination or a corrupted citation block within the database export used to generate the PDF.

comments.title: OCR Artifact in German Umlaut
comments.quote: David Ha and J¨urgen Schmidhuber. World models. arXiv:1803.10122, 2018.
comments.explanation: The character sequence 'J\"urgen' is clearly an OCR error or a formatting artifact representing the German letter 'ü'. The authors' surname is Schmidhuber, and the given name is Jürgen. The double ring/diaeresis is a common artifact when special characters (Unicode) are not properly recognized during PDF-to-text conversion, rendering an umlaut as a combining character and a ring.

comments.title: Garbage Text in Citation Author Field
comments.quote: SM Ali Eslami, Danilo Jimenez Rezende, Frederic Besse...
comments.explanation: The author field begins with 'SM' before 'Ali Eslami'. Given the adjacent citation for 'Science, 360...' by Ali Eslami (which is the correct journal for this paper), 'SM' is almost certainly an artifact from the PDF source (perhaps a scanning artifact from a column header or a repeated journal abbreviation like 'Science Monthly' or 'Science Magazine'). It is not part of the author's name.

comments.title: Garbage Character in Author Name
comments.quote: ... Zeming\' Lin, Florian Richoux, and Nicolas Usunier.
comments.explanation: The character 'Zeming\'' contains a hanging accent mark (grave accent) that is not part of the standard spelling of the name (likely 'Zeming Lin' or similar). This appears to be a layout artifact where a quotation mark or curly bracket from the PDF layout bled into the text stream.
